summary:
The cave systems of Palawan island in the southwestern Philippines — particularly Tabon Cave and the associated Lipuun Point cave complex — preserve an important record of early human occupation in island Southeast Asia. Tabon Cave yielded human skeletal fragments (the Tabon Man remains) dated to approximately 47,000–24,000 BCE, establishing human presence in the Philippines during the late Pleistocene when lower sea levels during the glacial maximum exposed land connections between the islands.

For the period 10,000–8,000 BCE, the Palawan cave systems were occupied by hunter-gatherers adapting to the changing environmental conditions of the early Holocene, including the rising sea levels accompanying the [[event:1262|end of the Ice Age]] that were progressively flooding the broad continental shelves of [[event:1318|Sundaland]] and isolating the Philippine islands. The inhabitants of Tabon Cave exploited marine, riverine, and forest resources, and their material culture shows continuity with earlier Palaeolithic traditions while beginning to incorporate new techniques. The Palawan cave sequence provides important evidence for the human populations of the Philippines — ancestors of both the Negrito peoples and later Austronesian settlers — across the critical period of environmental transformation at the close of the Pleistocene.
